With AI infrastructure spending projected to reach trillions within the decade, whoever establishes the benchmark contract owns the reference price for the most contested input in tech.

title: India’s Udaan secures $160 million in new funding as it races to fix its balance sheet before an IPO
blurb: Udaan raised $160M through a mix of fresh equity, new debt, and converting existing bonds into shares to clean up its balance sheet before an IPO it expects within two years. The round, backed by Lightspeed and M&G, comes weeks after the Indian B2B ecommerce firm defaulted on $170M in convertible notes that matured June 30. It marks the third raise in five months and lands at under a $2B valuation, down from a $3B peak, a reminder that even a company claiming 70% of India's B2B ecommerce volume has to repair its books before public markets will take it.

title: Where Meta’s WhatsApp agent can actually win
blurb: Meta made its Business AI agent available to companies worldwide on June 3, a WhatsApp bot that answers questions, recommends products, books appointments, and qualifies leads on top of a network past three billion monthly users. Paid messaging on WhatsApp crossed a $2B annual run rate in Q4 2025, and click-to-WhatsApp ad revenue grew 60% year over year. But China already ran this experiment: WeChat had the users and the wallet and still lost commerce to Douyin, because a chat window creates no demand and delivers no goods, so the agent's real edge is appointments and local services, not shipping.

title: $500M ARR, 60 Engineers, Cash-Flow Positive: How Higgsfield Actually Runs, With CEO Alex Mashrabov
blurb: Higgsfield crossed a $500M annualized revenue run rate in June, up from $200M at the end of 2025 and cash-flow positive, on a team of about 150 with roughly 60 in core engineering. That works out to around $5M in ARR per engineer against a typical $2M, and the platform didn't exist before March 2025. The efficiency comes from a deliberate bet: pair engineers with 70-plus filmmakers who generate every asset on the platform and tell the engineers exactly where the models break, then the engineers fix it.

title: Neko Health raises $700M as demand grows for preventive health scans
blurb: Neko Health raised $700M in a Series C led by Lightspeed, less than 18 months after its $260M Series B, as demand for preventive full-body scans keeps climbing. The Daniel Ek-backed company sells a 60-minute, radiation-free scan priced at £299 that captures millions of data points and flags risks for diabetes, stroke, and heart disease on the spot. The capital funds a first US expansion, and the signal for founders is a consumer health market willing to pay out of pocket for prevention, with most members returning after their first scan.

title: Revolut and ElevenLabs among first bets from Aspire11’s €100 million capital deployment
blurb: Prague-based Aspire11 deployed the first €100M of its €515M fund into a portfolio that includes Revolut, Databricks, ElevenLabs, Vinted, and Baseten, all drawn from European pension capital. The bet is structural: the median time to IPO has stretched from about seven years to 11 over the past decade, so most of a company's value now compounds while it is still private and out of reach for pensions stuck in public markets. Aspire11's tranche is part of at least €392M in related private-markets and pension-infrastructure announcements across Europe this year.

title: So Far, 2026 Is A Solid Year For Cybersecurity Startup Funding
blurb: Cybersecurity funding is holding at historically high levels even as AI megarounds dominate the headlines, with security and privacy startups pulling in $10.6B globally in the first half of 2026. Q2 cooled to $4.4B, down about 30% from both the prior quarter and a year ago, though the two quarters before it were unusually strong, so the dip reads as reversion rather than a warning. The capital is still concentrating at the top: eight rounds topped $100M, led by Cyera's $600M raise at a $12B valuation for AI-agent security tooling.

title: An inside look at Mutiny's growth engine
blurb: Matt Ratchford, the Growth Marketing Lead at Mutiny, is documenting week-by-week how he builds a growth engine on a small team, and the value is in what most build-in-public content leaves out. His posts include the misses, stay specific, and describe an AI-forward workflow that a normal operator can actually copy rather than a top-1% power-user flex. The pattern to steal is the "Gen Marketer" mode: set the strategy and goals yourself, then execute with AI on the parts that used to need a bigger team.

title: Paris-based Mio emerges from stealth with €1.9 million to build an AI colleague that lives inside Slack
blurb: Paris-based Mio raised €1.9M pre-seed to build an AI colleague inside Slack that acts on your company's data, not just answers.

title: $700m backing for Spotify founder’s ground-breaking Neko Health
blurb: Neko Health, the preventive-health startup co-founded by Spotify's Daniel Ek, raised $700M in Series C led by Lightspeed and O.G. Venture Partners, up from a $260M Series B just 18 months earlier. Its £299 body scan captures millions of data points across skin, heart, and circulation in 60 minutes, then delivers results on-site with a clinician. More than 100,000 people in the UK and Sweden have already had one, with 350,000 more on the waitlist, and the vertically integrated model, all hardware and software built in-house, is what investors are betting scales.

title: What are Forward Deployed Engineers, and why are they so in demand? (2025)
blurb: Forward Deployed Engineers, the software-plus-sales-plus-platform role Palantir pioneered, are now the hottest hire in tech, and startups from OpenAI to Ramp are staffing up fast. The job embeds an engineer inside customer teams to integrate the product, then rotates them back to core engineering, which makes it a near-perfect fit for shipping LLM and AI deployments. Ramp already runs about 15 FDEs in pods, and a16z has called it the hottest job in tech.

title: Valarian raises £37m to build UK sovereign AI infrastructure
blurb: Valarian raised £37m in a Series A led by NEA, taking total funding to $70m and marking NEA's first defence and dual-use bet in Europe. The money goes toward rolling out UK-sovereign digital infrastructure across government, defence, and enterprise, with its ACRA platform letting organisations run AI while keeping sensitive data inside their own environment. Sovereign control was once the preserve of defence and heavily regulated institutions, but as AI embeds everywhere, the ability to keep data and models in-house is becoming a baseline requirement for a much wider set of buyers.

title: NYC-based Hadrius, which provides AI-native compliance tools for financial services, raised a $22M Series A led by CRV with participation from Y Combinator (Ryan Lawler/Axios)
blurb: Hadrius raised a $22M Series A led by CRV, with Y Combinator joining, to build AI-native compliance tools for financial services firms. Compliance is one of the clearest wedges for AI in regulated industries, where the work is document-heavy, rules-driven, and expensive to staff with humans. The bet is that a startup native to AI can undercut incumbent GRC vendors on cost while covering more of the audit and monitoring workflow, in a market where the buyers face real regulatory downside for getting it wrong.

title: Sovereign AI infrastructure startup Valarian raises $50M to help nations secure their defense systems
blurb: Valarian raised $50M in a Series A led by New Enterprise Associates, its first bet on a European defense startup, to sell nations sovereign control over the AI systems running their military infrastructure. The pitch lands because European defense spending topped €392B in 2025 while the AI underneath it increasingly runs on a handful of US firms that can revoke access at any time. Valarian's ACRA platform enforces workload-level isolation over sensitive compute, and the broader signal is that "sovereign AI" is becoming a fundable category as governments treat vendor dependence as a national-security risk.

title: Tom Blomfield is taking a leave of absence from YC to join Anthropic
blurb: Tom Blomfield, the Monzo cofounder and YC group partner, is taking a leave of absence from YC to join Anthropic's compute team, working under Tom Brown. He framed the move around recursive self-improvement, arguing that as AI starts improving itself, access to compute becomes one of the field's most important constraints to solve. The pull is telling: one of the most visible startup mentors is leaving the incubator that shapes founders to work on infrastructure at a frontier lab, a signal of where the leverage in AI now sits.

title: Expat Startmate startup Gridcog powers up on $14 million Series A
blurb: Energy-modelling startup Gridcog raised $14M in a Series A led by ABB, with Axpo, DNV Ventures, and VERBUND X Ventures joining alongside AlbionVC and the Clean Energy Finance Corporation. The strategic backers matter more than the number: industrial electrification, energy consulting, and large-scale generation and trading firms are all betting that rigorous flexibility modelling becomes core infrastructure for the energy transition, not a nice-to-have. Gridcog has already modelled more than 16,000 projects across 40-plus markets for the likes of Shell and Vestas, turning weeks of spreadsheet work into hours of analysis.

title: ABB, Axpo, DNV, and VERBUND back Gridcog in a $10M round to enhance modelling of critical energy projects
blurb: Gridcog, a London-based energy flexibility modelling platform, raised a $10M Series A led by ABB, with Axpo, DNV Ventures, and VERBUND X Ventures joining. What makes the round notable is that all four new backers are major energy companies that already run their own project decisions through Gridcog's software, spanning industrial electrification, energy consulting, and large-scale generation and trading. Gridcog has now modelled more than 16,000 energy projects across 40-plus countries, a signal that rigorous modelling is becoming infrastructure for the energy transition as grid connections tighten and projects shift from single wind or solar farms to hybrid, multi-asset sites.

title: The ex-Palantir and Dropbox duo helping the likes of ElevenLabs, Notion and Wiz ship AI-generated code faster
blurb: Meticulous, founded by ex-Palantir and Dropbox brothers Gabriel and Quentin Spencer-Harper, raised a $15M Series A led by Chemistry to help developers trust AI-generated code. The tool maps every edge case in a codebase, then simulates user flows before and after each change and returns a report of thumbnails showing exactly how the change affects each screen and user type. It tests frontend code today, with full-stack coverage planned, and ARR grew five times in the past year.

title: Europe’s real founder factories are not Google or unicorns, but early-stage startups you have never heard of
blurb: Europe's most prolific founder factories aren't Google, Spotify, or the big unicorns, but early-stage startups almost nobody has heard of. An Antler report tracking where founders come from finds that small, obscure companies produce more second-time founders per capita than the marquee names everyone assumes are the talent pipeline. The takeaway for anyone hiring or investing: the people who leave a scrappy 30-person startup are disproportionately the ones who go on to build the next one, so pedigree from a famous logo is a weaker signal than it looks.

title: Startup Spotlight: Hedgehog bets that open-source networking will power the next generation of AI clouds
blurb: Rising AI cloud bills are pushing more companies to pull GPU workloads out of public clouds and into their own data centers, and networking has become the choke point. Seattle startup Hedgehog, founded by Cisco veteran Marc Austin and backed by $11M in seed funding, sells open-source software that lets platform and DevOps teams stand up hyperscaler-grade GPU fabrics in hours instead of months, on open hardware with no vendor lock-in. The buyers rarely turn out to be network engineers, and many want to resell that capacity to their own customers the way a cloud provider does.

title: Cursor IDE Auto-Executes Malicious Code in Poisoned Repos
blurb: Mindgard disclosed a vulnerability in Cursor that auto-executes a malicious git.exe planted at a repository root, running arbitrary code with the developer's privileges the moment the poisoned project is opened. The firm reported the flaw in December and repeatedly since, but Cursor never accepted or fixed it, leaving the path-resolution behavior exploitable in poisoned-repo attacks against the latest tested version.

title: Cursor 0day: When Full Disclosure Becomes the Only Protection Left
blurb: Mindgard disclosed a Cursor zero-day that gives arbitrary code execution on Windows when a developer opens any repository containing a malicious git.exe in the project root, with no clicks, prompts, or warnings. The firm reported it in December 2025, but more than six months and 197+ releases later it remains unpatched, leaving VMs, Windows Sandbox, or AppLocker deny rules as the only mitigations.

title: Bonsai 27B: A 27B-Class model that runs on a phone
blurb: Prism ML released Bonsai 27B, a 27B-class multimodal model based on Qwen3.6 that ships in ternary (5.9GB) and 1-bit (3.9GB) variants under Apache 2.0, with the 1-bit build fitting an iPhone 17 Pro. The low-bit weights run end to end with a 262K context and retain 90-95% of the full-precision baseline across a 15-benchmark suite.

title: The Tower Keeps Rising
blurb: Using Bruegel's Tower of Babel as a frame, the author argues that AI agents make individual developers dramatically faster at changing a codebase, but large projects have never been bottlenecked by code output. They are limited by shared understanding of a system's concepts, boundaries, and invariants, and much of that understanding was previously maintained by the friction of code review and cross-team conversation that agents now let developers bypass.

title: DSLs Enable Reliable Use of LLMs
blurb: Martin Fowler argues that domain-specific languages and abstractions give LLMs the clear boundaries they need to generate exactly the intended code, acting as a harness that constrains output from the start. Using Tickloom, a DSL for modeling distributed system behavior, he shows a two-phase workflow: partner with the LLM to iteratively build the DSL, then use natural language to drive it, with the DSL itself serving as the source of truth.

title: Upwind links compromise of multiple AsyncAPI npm packages to coordinated attack on software release process
blurb: Upwind published findings from an investigation into a coordinated supply-chain campaign that compromised multiple official AsyncAPI npm packages through two separate GitHub repositories, several release branches, and abused OIDC publishing identities. Rather than the usual preinstall or postinstall hooks, the malicious code ran during normal package imports, evading tools that focus on monitoring installation.

title: How Claude's values vary by model and language
blurb: Anthropic studied how the values Claude expresses shift across different models and languages, compressing 3,000-plus observed values into four axes such as Deference vs. Caution and Warmth vs. Rigor. The analysis quantifies how character training and fine-tuning decisions move a model along each axis, and shows Claude's responses vary measurably across the top 20 languages used on Claude.ai.

title: Trust but Verify? Uncovering the Security Debt of Autonomous Coding Agents
blurb: An empirical study of 4,022 agent-generated pull requests found that 38.9% contain at least one security smell, with supply chain integrity issues making up 82.3% and hard-coded credentials accounting for 99.6% of critical-severity findings. The authors note that human collaborators introduced 67.6% of genuine leaked secrets and that existing automated and manual reviews missed 81.1% of them before merge, pointing to eroding developer vigilance in AI-assisted workflows.

title: A broken DNSSEC rollover took down .AL. Now 1.1.1.1 tells you when validation is bypassed
blurb: A failed DNSSEC key rollover on Albania's .AL ccTLD on July 3 broke validation and made every .AL domain unreachable through validating resolvers like Cloudflare's 1.1.1.1, which spec-compliantly returned SERVFAIL until an NTA was applied to suspend validation. This time Cloudflare also attached a new Extended DNS Error code to every affected response, so clients can finally tell when an answer was served without DNSSEC validation rather than being spoofed.

title: The Same TypeScript Costs 73% More Tokens on Claude Than GPT
blurb: The post measures how many tokens each frontier vendor's tokenizer produces from identical files and finds Anthropic's newest tokenizer (Sonnet 5, Opus 4.8, Fable 5) emits 1.36–1.73x GPT's count on the same bytes, with TypeScript the worst case at 1.73x. Because list prices are fixed per token, that gap turns Opus 4.8's advertised $5/$25 into an effective $7.50/$37.50 on identical input, measuring input tokenization alone before output verbosity and caching.

title: Dependabot version updates introduce default package cooldown
blurb: Dependabot version updates now wait until a new release has been on its registry for three days before opening an update PR, a default that applies across all supported ecosystems on github.com with no configuration required. The cooldown gives supply-chain signals time to surface before a bad release merges, and it skips security updates entirely so critical fixes still open immediately. Set a custom window or opt out via the cooldown option in dependabot.yml.

title: How I use HTMX with Go
blurb: Alex Edwards walks through his patterns for using HTMX with Go's html/template package, focusing on structuring templates to return both partial and full-page HTML responses. The post covers handling redirects and errors over HTMX, the standard configuration settings he applies, and builds a small user-filtering app to illustrate keeping interactivity server-rendered with minimal JavaScript.

title: Codex starts encrypting sub-agent prompts
blurb: A merged change (#26210) encrypts Codex's MultiAgentV2 message payloads, storing only ciphertext and leaving the human-readable task and message fields empty in local rollout history and parent-side traces. The regression report argues privacy hardening shouldn't erase auditability, proposing a separate unencrypted audit copy so engineers can still answer what task a spawn_agent call gave a child agent when debugging after the fact.

title: Coding agents think ahead of time
blurb: This paper shows that language models inside coding agents linearly encode program state in their residual streams, with logistic-regression probes decoding whether the current code parses, passes tests, or introduces regressions at AUC up to 0.83 across two models and benchmarks. More surprising, the probes predict the outcome of future edits up to roughly 25 steps before they are written to disk, a "latent programming horizon" that transfers across benchmarks without retraining.

title: Form, Not Content? A Preregistered, Placebo-Controlled Evaluation of Learned Error-Conditioned Self-Repair Through Prompts and Weights in Frozen Small Code Models
blurb: This paper introduces PoPE, a preregistered, placebo-controlled method for testing whether frozen small code models (0.5-1.5B) actually use execution error feedback when retrying failed programs, rather than just reacting to the prompt's shape. Across both a prompt channel and adapter-training weight channel, content-ablated placebos matched or beat the live error arms (12 vs 10 unlocks, and an 8-8 tie), so error content showed no operational benefit over its scaffold.

title: Taming the Drift: Context-aware Repair of Dockerfile Drift during Software Evolution
blurb: Cadre repairs Dockerfile drift by building a Context-aware Dependency Graph that links each instruction to its file-level and inter-instruction dependencies, then generating targeted patches only from the causally relevant context. On a new 1,040-instance benchmark it hits a 35.22% repair rate, 1.24x the best LLM baseline, while keeping 95% of prompts under 30k tokens to avoid the prompt-overflow failures that sink diff-only methods.

title: Show HN: Beautiful Type Erasure with C++26 Reflection
blurb: rjk::duck uses C++26 reflection to implement type erasure from a single interface declaration, replacing the 100+ lines of hand-written boilerplate or heavyweight libraries like Boost.TypeErasure and Folly.Poly. The single-header library supports owning and non-owning semantics, operators, interface composition, and third-party adapters, and the post walks through the tag generation, vtable codegen, and overload resolution that make it work, though it currently builds only on gcc with -std=c++26 -freflection.

title: mariadb-server: Upgrading to 10.11.18-0+deb12u1 enables systemd socket activation unconditionally
blurb: A Debian security update to mariadb-server 10.11.18-0+deb12u1 turns on systemd socket activation unconditionally, overriding operators who had deliberately disabled it. The change can restart or reconfigure the listener on upgrade, so admins running MariaDB on non-default sockets or with hardened network settings should audit their unit files before applying the patch.

title: Mystra: Declarative Dynamic Taint Analysis via Shadow Virtual Machine
blurb: This paper introduces Mystra, a declarative language for dynamic taint analysis that decouples taint semantics from runtime instrumentation by running a Shadow Virtual Machine alongside the host and compiling rules ahead of time to constant-dispatch binaries. Their V8 implementation, Shar, hits 95.5% recall with zero false positives across 493 Node.js CVEs, at 1.85x overhead versus vanilla Node and 22.7x less than NodeMedic-FINE.

title: Solving 20 Erdős Problems with 20 Codex Accounts Running in Parallel
blurb: Star Fleet Math is a Mac desktop app that runs up to 20 agentic "starships" in parallel, each on its own 60-vCPU server with a dedicated GPT-5.6 instance, to attack open Erdős problems and formalize proofs in Lean 4. Each starship can burst to thousands of vCPUs or H100s for search, queries a Lean 4 premise corpus via vector search, and compounds verified lemmas in a dependency-graph memory.

title: In defense of polyfills
blurb: Lea Verou defends polyfills as a net positive for the web after WHATWG editor Anne van Kesteren called them harmful in a mutation-observer proposal meeting. She frames the debate as cost-benefit rather than absolute, arguing that rare cases where a polyfill got too popular too soon and constrained a native API's design space are post-mortem material, not grounds to discourage polyfilling wholesale.

title: Are you telling me a readonly property is wrecking my performance?
blurb: Debugging a slowdown in Letta Desktop, the author traced it to auto-scrolling chat via `el.scrollTop = el.scrollHeight`, where reading scrollHeight forces a synchronous style and layout recalc on every streamed message. The post walks through Chromium's scrollHeight implementation to show the property is computed on access rather than cached, and fixes it by assigning a large constant that the browser clamps instead of measuring.

title: Line-Anchored Feedback Cuts Token Costs and Improves Correctness in AI Code Editing
blurb: FileMark is a VSCodium extension that exports inline code comments as structured, line-anchored feedback to guide AI code edits, replacing holistic prompts. In a paired experiment it cut generated tokens by 22% on Claude Opus and 58% on Claude Sonnet, reaching 24-80% on files over 100 lines, while lifting correctness by 2 points pooled and tripling local-model accuracy on large files when the harness applies patches.

title: How I Cut an AI Agent's Token Use by 94%
blurb: The author replaced most of an LLM-driven Agent Skill with deterministic Python after the workflow crystallized, keeping the model only for the two steps that need judgment: picking a candidate post to resurface and drafting the LinkedIn copy. Compiling the natural-language skill into a thin bootloader over a Python program cut token use by 94%, since fetching sources, building the content inventory, and applying recent-post filters never needed reasoning in the first place.

title: Software Supply Chains are Dead: Use-Case-Oriented Regeneration
blurb: The authors argue that generative AI and supply chain attacks have inverted the build-vs-reuse calculus, proposing "use-case-oriented regeneration" that synthesizes only the slice of a dependency a repository actually exercises. Across 180 repository-dependency pairs, an agentic workflow preserved 99.8% of observed behavior while cutting the exported API surface by 93%, trading external trust for locally verifiable, repo-specific code.

title: Code Yellow, Code Red
blurb: The author recounts declaring the first Code Yellow at Provet after months of infrastructure degradation eroded customer trust, setting a target of zero downtime for eight consecutive weeks and hitting it with only one brief incident. The post defines Code Yellow and Code Red, traces the terms back to Google, and offers a template for running one: how to structure the work, when to call and escalate, and the failure modes to watch for.

title: How to stop Claude from saying load-bearing
blurb: Johanna Larsson uses a Claude Code MessageDisplay hook to rewrite the model's overused stock phrases, swapping "load-bearing" for "cooked" and "you're absolutely right" for "I'm a complete clown" via a small Python script fed through settings.json. The point is less the joke than the mechanism: the hook intercepts rendered output at display time, so any regex substitution runs on every message without touching the model itself.

title: Foundational Constraint Solving for Expressive Refinement Typing
blurb: FLEX is a foundational Constrained Horn Clause solver written in Lean that shrinks the trusted base to the Lean kernel, replacing an unverified SMT solver with tactics that emit kernel-checkable proofs. The authors compose it with verified CHC generators for imperative and refinement-typed languages, and show it discharges 95.7% of the CHCs in FLUX's benchmark suite while proving functional correctness of low-level Rust libraries.

title: WebMCP for Documentation
blurb: WorkOS added WebMCP support to its documentation site, exposing structured tools through `document.modelContext` so AI agents can call the page's own search, navigation, and code samples directly instead of scraping rendered HTML and guessing. The approach avoids standing up a separate MCP server, which would duplicate the docs' content and drift out of sync, since WebMCP annotates the live DOM agents and humans already share.

title: Multi-Perspective Agentic Program Repair via Code Property Graphs and Temporal Execution Graphs
blurb: CT-Repair is an agentic program-repair framework that encodes static and dynamic evidence as queryable Code Property Graphs and compact Temporal Execution Graphs, then runs three FSM-guided agents that reason about each bug from static, dynamic, and hybrid perspectives. On 854 Java bugs from Defects4J v3.0 it fixes 489, with the three perspectives combined repairing 99 more bugs than the strongest single one and execution filtering trimming candidate method scope by 94.85%.

title: Your Dropbox is now a skill server
blurb: sx 2.0 shipped as a native desktop app for Mac, Windows, and Linux that distributes AI skills through a shared folder in Dropbox, Google Drive, OneDrive, or iCloud, with no git or terminal required. Its new vault format v2 stores each asset as plain markdown on disk with version history alongside, and the app syncs skills natively into Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, Codex, Gemini, Cline, and Kiro.

title: With AI Agents, Attention is the New Bottleneck
blurb: AI agents removed execution as the constraint on shipping software, but the author argues that just exposed the next bottleneck: the human work of deciding what to build, judging output, and catching which parallel task is about to fail. The fix is to manage cognitive load deliberately by automating low-stakes decisions through lint rules, defaults, and agent instructions, and by estimating attention cost per task the way teams already estimate points.

title: Why You Should Tune Code Before Your Garbage Collector
blurb: Using a JLBH event-to-response latency benchmark on Chronicle-FIX at 50K/s, the author shows that removing one redundant SLF4J log line per message drops p99.99 latency by three orders of magnitude and flips which garbage collector wins. The takeaway is to tune allocation and redundant work before picking a collector, since IO placement and logging policy swamp the GC choice at the tail.

title: Part 1: Why I Rarely Use useEffect Anymore (and what I use instead)
blurb: The author argues that most useEffect calls are unnecessary, since the hook is meant only for synchronizing with external systems like network requests, timers, and subscriptions. Deriving values, syncing props into state, and filtering lists should happen during render or in a useMemo, avoiding the double-render and dual-source-of-truth bugs that effects introduce.

title: MorphoHDL: A minimalistic language for growing circuits
blurb: MorphoHDL is a minimalistic hardware description language that specifies circuits through local growth rules rather than explicit gate-by-gate wiring, letting a structure develop outward from a seed. The project page pairs the language with interactive demos that let you grow, pause, and re-grow circuits step by step, framing morphogenesis as an alternative to conventional static netlist descriptions.

title: GraphPolaris: A System for Query, Analysis, and Visualization of Graph Databases
blurb: GraphPolaris is a no-code visual analytics system for querying and visualizing graph databases, built around GPQL, a formal query grammar that generates valid, database-agnostic queries from user interactions rather than hand-written Cypher or Gremlin. The authors position it as the graph equivalent of Tableau or Looker, targeting fraud detection, supply-chain, and social-network analysis where tabular BI can't reason over nodes and edges. Evaluation spans telecom and supply-chain case studies and a 22-month formative study with analysts.

title: FAIR GraphRAG: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Approach for Semantic Data Analysis
blurb: This paper introduces FAIR GraphRAG, a graph-based retrieval framework that makes FAIR Digital Objects the nodes of the knowledge graph, so each node carries core data, metadata, persistent identifiers, and semantic links. LLMs handle schema construction and automated extraction of content and metadata from source data. Applied to a gastroenterology RNA-sequencing dataset, the authors report gains in question-answering accuracy, coverage, and explainability, with the largest improvements on complex queries that lean on metadata and ontology links.

title: GRAFT: Graph-Matched Retrieval and Fusion of Tables in Data Lakes
blurb: GRAFT reframes table retrieval in data lakes as a graph-matching problem, pairing a query-derived intent graph against a heterogeneous lake graph so that joinable and unionable tables are pulled together rather than scored in isolation. The authors argue that point-wise retrieval hands downstream data agents fragmented evidence they cannot integrate. On Spider and BIRD adapted to the tabular lake setting, GRAFT reports 7.8% higher F1 and 10.6% higher Sufficiency than the strongest baseline while keeping search efficient.

title: Policy-Conditioned Constrained Decoding for Column-Level Access Control in Text-to-SQL
blurb: This paper introduces PCC-SQL, a text-to-SQL system that enforces column-level access policies during decoding rather than by refusing queries after the fact. It defines policy over how a column is used (output, filter, aggregation) and applies a per-token logits mask that deterministically blocks violations in a single pass. Across three benchmarks and three open models, it hit a 0% leakage rate and up to 88.7% coverage on Spider-CU while staying within 10% of direct-prompting token cost.

title: This optical illusion font was created to baffle AI, and it actually works (for now)
blurb: Ghost Font hides text in animated drifting dots that humans read through motion but multimodal AI, parsing frame by frame, can't.

title: 'The world is deserving of better design': Koto's Jowey Roden on his mission to make quality typefaces affordable
blurb: Koto, the branding agency behind identity work for Amazon, Google, Netflix, and WhatsApp, launched CcType, its first commercial font foundry, with typefaces priced for freelancers and small studios rather than enterprise clients. Co-founder Jowey Roden frames the foundry as an extension of the studio's Seasoned learning hub, applying the same access-widening instinct to a paid product built to put a decade of type craft within reach of smaller budgets.

title: Canva Code 2.0 just made vibe coding way less intimidating for everyone
blurb: Canva released Canva Code 2.0 to all users, combining prompt-based app and site generation with its editor so output pulls from a team's existing brand kit, fonts, and photos rather than defaulting to generic AI-prototype styling. Users can start from a prompt, 50-plus templates, or imported HTML, then edit collaboratively in real time and publish to a custom or Canva-hosted domain.

title: I tried Maxon's free After Effects alternative, and I don't want to go back
blurb: Maxon released Autograph, a free GPU-powered motion graphics and compositing tool positioned as an After Effects alternative, spanning both motion design/animation and VFX/compositing workflows. The interface leans closer to a 3D package than a 2D design app, so After Effects users face a learning curve, but the timeline-based layer editing will feel familiar and the perpetual-free pricing sidesteps Adobe's subscription lock-in.

title: Colorado house by Studio B and Studio Brother John appears to "hover over the land"
blurb: Studio B Architecture + Interiors and Studio Brother John completed a Boulder, Colorado house that cantilevers over its sloped site to appear to hover above the land, replacing a home destroyed in a wildfire. The design leans on the mountainous terrain rather than flattening it, using the elevated volume to preserve the site's natural grade and frame views across the Front Range.

title: Designing Agent-Ready Websites for AI Web Agents: A Framework for Machine Readability, Actionability, and Decision Reliability
blurb: This paper introduces the "agent-ready website," a design framework that restructures e-commerce pages around machine readability, action cues, and decision-reliability signals so AI browser agents can extract, compare, and complete purchases. In a 300-run test across three agent models, the agent-ready prototype hit an 89.3% strict success rate versus 49.3% for the human-oriented baseline, while cutting average steps from 9.31 to 6.49.

title: GPT-5.6 is Now Available in Figma Make
blurb: Figma added OpenAI's GPT-5.6 as a model option in Figma Make, its prompt-to-prototype builder, citing faster and higher-fidelity first-pass output on complex UIs. The model self-heals on build errors rather than stalling, and stays faithful to existing Figma Design files, carrying over layout, spacing, styling, and working interactions like playback controls when generating from a spec.

title: Heavily stylistic approach to stadium design "should die down" says Populous global director Jonathan Mallie
blurb: Populous global director Jonathan Mallie argues that the era of heavily stylistic, icon-driven stadium design should give way to buildings shaped by urban planning, computational design, and hard economics. He frames the modern stadium as a year-round civic anchor rather than a sculptural gesture, where flexible programming and district integration matter more than a signature silhouette.

title: What 32 design leaders do when told to move faster
blurb: A survey of 32 design leaders finds that AI has collapsed the generation phase of design work, cutting production time from days to hours and prompting companies to demand ever-shorter timelines in return. The author argues the response is not to keep accelerating but to shift the value of the role toward judgment and problem framing, the parts that speed cannot compress.

title: Decibel's Circular-Material 3D Printed Chair
blurb: Decibel 3D-printed a chair from Filaflex, a bio-based compostable filament from Italian materials company Balena, using robotic large-format additive manufacturing that eliminates molds and builds structural ribbing through controlled deposition. The wicker-like piece, which won a SIT Furniture Design Award, argues that production-ready furniture can skip fossil-based plastics without giving up sculptural form or everyday durability.

title: TS-2026-009: Insecure argument handling in Tailscale SSH permitted root access
blurb: Tailscale patched TS-2026-009, an insecure argument-handling flaw in Tailscale SSH that let a user with SSH access to a Linux node escalate to root in violation of ACLs by connecting with a username beginning with a dash. Because these usernames were passed to getent(1), a login as `-i` was parsed as a flag that dumped the passwd file and opened an interactive root session; upgrade to version 1.98.9 or newer.

title: Progress confirms ShareFile zero-day flaw behind Storage Zone shutdown
blurb: Progress Software confirmed that a high-severity path traversal zero-day in ShareFile Storage Zone Controller (versions 5.x and 6.x) triggered last week's emergency shutdown, allowing an authenticated admin to read arbitrary files, write attacker-controlled content, and enumerate the server filesystem. Progress released versions 5.12.5 and 6.0.2 and patched the flaw before public disclosure; a reserved CVE will publish in two weeks, and no customer breach has been identified.

title: Two SonicWall SMA 1000 Zero-Days Exploited, One Could Enable Admin Commands
blurb: SonicWall warned of active exploitation of two SMA 1000 zero-days: CVE-2026-15409 (CVSS 10.0), an unauthenticated SSRF, and CVE-2026-15410 (CVSS 7.2), a post-auth code injection in the Appliance Management Console that lets an authenticated attacker run OS commands as administrator. Patch to 12.4.3-03453 or 12.5.0-02835 and check extraweb_access.log for /__api__/login and /wsproxy requests; CISA's KEV deadline for FCEB agencies is July 17.

title: SonicWall warns of SMA1000 flaws exploited in zero-day attacks, patch now
blurb: SonicWall confirmed active zero-day exploitation of two SMA1000 flaws, led by CVE-2026-15409, a critical (CVSS 10.0) unauthenticated SSRF in the Appliance Work Place interface, alongside CVE-2026-15410, a post-auth (CVSS 7.2) OS command injection in the Management Console. Patch affected 6210, 7210, and 8200v appliances to hotfix 12.4.3-03453 or 12.5.0-02835, then hunt the published IOCs for rogue /__api__/login routes in extraweb_access.log and conf.json.

title: Unpatched Claude for Chrome Flaw Lets Extensions Read Gmail, Calendar
blurb: Manifold disclosed two unpatched vulnerabilities in Claude for Chrome, Anthropic's agentic browser extension, that let a malicious extension forge user clicks and drive Claude into reading a victim's Gmail, Google Docs, and calendar without genuine approval. The flaws survive an earlier ClaudeBleed mitigation across all eight releases since the May 21 report, including 1.0.80, and run silently for users who enabled the extension's autonomous "Act without asking" mode.

title: SAP Patches CVSS 9.9 NetWeaver ABAP Flaw That Could Expose or Modify Data
blurb: SAP patched CVE-2026-44747 (CVSS 9.9), an out-of-bounds write in NetWeaver Application Server ABAP that lets an authenticated attacker trigger memory corruption to read, modify, or take systems offline. The July release also fixed CVE-2026-44761 (CVSS 9.1), default OAuth credentials in Commerce Cloud that grant unauthenticated API access; patch now or apply Onapsis's SICF ICF-node workaround where full patching isn't yet feasible.

title: Grok Build was uploading entire Git repositories to xAI’s cloud, including committed secrets
blurb: A security researcher publishing as cereblab showed on July 12 that xAI's Grok Build CLI (v0.2.93) uploaded developers' entire tracked repositories, including full Git history, committed secrets, and API keys, to a Google Cloud Storage bucket despite a privacy toggle marketed as blocking all transmission. Musk confirmed the uploads and said prior data would be deleted, but no independent audit has verified the deletion or the newly added zero-retention policy.

title: Cursor 0day: When Full Disclosure Becomes the Only Protection Left
blurb: An unpatched zero-day in Cursor lets a malicious git.exe planted in a repository root execute automatically on Windows when a developer opens the project, with no clicks, prompts, or warnings, yielding arbitrary code execution that repeats on a cadence. Mindgard reported it on December 15, 2025, but it remains present 197+ versions later, so treat untrusted repos as hostile and open them only in Windows Sandbox or a disposable VM until Cursor ships a fix.

title: AI used to help plan the break-in, now it’s doing the break-in
blurb: Check Point's AI Security Report 2026 documented intrusions over the past year in which AI ran exploitation workflows autonomously, generating thousands of commands across dozens of sessions with minimal human direction. The report also flags a persistent jailbreak vector against coding agents: malicious instructions planted in auto-loaded files like CLAUDE.md stay active every session until the file is removed, while exploit development now lands within hours of public disclosure.

title: 11 Old Microsoft-Signed Linux UEFI Shims Could Let Attackers Bypass Secure Boot
blurb: ESET disclosed 11 old Microsoft-signed Linux UEFI shim bootloaders (mostly shim 0.9 and earlier) that let an attacker execute untrusted code during boot, bypassing Secure Boot on any system trusting the Microsoft Corporation UEFI CA 2011 certificate regardless of installed OS. Microsoft revoked the affected shims in its June 2026 Patch Tuesday update, so apply the DBX revocations to block deployment of bootkits like BlackLotus, Bootkitty, and HybridPetya.

title: Microsoft’s Secure Boot has been broken for a decade and no one noticed until now
blurb: ESET researchers found 11 UEFI Secure Boot shims, at least one dating to 2013, that Microsoft signed but never revoked despite known flaws, letting an attacker with a copy of any old shim bypass Secure Boot without a new exploit. The unrevoked binaries affect both Windows and Linux devices and enable persistent bootkits that survive OS reinstalls and drive swaps, so defenders should apply the updated UEFI revocation (DBX) lists.

title: OAuth Client ID Spoofing Lets Attackers Validate Stolen Microsoft Entra Credentials
blurb: Proofpoint disclosed OAuth client ID spoofing, an evasion technique used by at least two threat actors, including UNK_CustomCloak, to enumerate accounts and validate stolen credentials against Microsoft Entra ID without generating a successful sign-in event. Attackers send ROPC-flow requests with syntactically valid but unregistered client IDs to the OAuth token endpoint, reading AADSTS error codes to confirm usernames and passwords while leaving only an application ID and no app name in sign-in logs.

title: New macOS malware steals passwords by posing as Apple’s crash-reporting tool
blurb: Jamf Threat Labs disclosed CrashStealer, a native C++ macOS infostealer that impersonates Apple's crash-reporting tool to harvest browser credentials, Keychain data, password-manager stores, and cryptocurrency wallets before exfiltrating them AES-GCM-encrypted over libcurl. Delivery uses a notarized, Developer ID-signed DMG ("Werkbit Setup," team Emil Grigorov / WWB7JA7AQV) that passes Gatekeeper, so defenders should revoke trust for that signing identity and hunt for the werkbit[.]io domain and hidden bundle-copy persistence.

title: SonicWall SMA appliances targeted in zero-day attacks (CVE-2026-15409, CVE-2026-15410)
blurb: SonicWall patched two actively exploited zero-days in its SMA 1000 Series appliances: CVE-2026-15409, an unauthenticated SSRF in the Work Place interface, and CVE-2026-15410, an admin-authenticated code injection in the Management Console, which attackers are chaining for RCE. Patching alone is insufficient; if IOCs are present, SonicWall advises re-imaging or redeploying appliances, rotating all user and admin passwords, and resetting TOTP tokens.

title: Microsoft July 2026 Patch Tuesday fixes massive 570 flaws, 3 zero-days
blurb: Microsoft's July 2026 Patch Tuesday addresses a record 570 flaws, including three zero-days, two of them actively exploited and one publicly disclosed. Prioritize CVE-2026-56155, an actively exploited Active Directory Federation Services elevation-of-privilege bug that grants administrative access, alongside the 59 critical vulnerabilities (48 of them RCE) before working through the remaining 254 elevation-of-privilege fixes.

title: Microsoft Patches a Record 570 Security Flaws
blurb: Microsoft's July Patch Tuesday fixed a record 570 flaws, nearly triple last month's count, including roughly 60 critical bugs and three zero-days, two of which are already being exploited in the wild. Prioritize the two exploited elevation-of-privilege zero-days plus CVE-2026-48561, a 9.6-CVSS Copilot RCE that a malicious site can trigger by forcing Edge for Android to send crafted prompts.

title: Study of 85 Crypto Wallet Extensions Finds Address Leaks and Cross-Site Tracking Risks
blurb: KU Leuven's DistriNet group tested 85 popular browser-extension crypto wallets covering roughly 35 million Chrome Web Store installs and found five privacy weaknesses that let servers and websites link a user's separate addresses and track them across sites, deanonymizing "anonymous" identities on any page already holding a name or email. The leaks are by-design behavior, not exploits: 17 wallets (about 23 million installs) exposed cross-address links, and most vendors declined to treat the reported flaws as bugs.

title: Full Broker Takeover, No Login Required: Miggo Discovers Critical RabbitMQ Vulnerabilities Putting Application Data at Risk
blurb: Miggo disclosed two access-control flaws in RabbitMQ: CVE-2026-57219 (CVSS 8.7), an unauthenticated management endpoint that leaks the broker's OAuth client secret and enables full takeover in confidential-client setups (Auth0, Entra ID, Keycloak, UAA), and CVE-2026-57221 (CVSS 5.3), a passive-declare authorization bypass letting any logged-in user enumerate other tenants' queues. Both affect release lines from 3.13.0 and are patched in 4.3.0, 4.2.6, 4.1.11, 4.0.20, and 3.13.15.

title: RabbitMQ Flaws Could Leak OAuth Secrets and Expose Cross-Tenant Queue Metadata
blurb: Miggo disclosed two RabbitMQ access-control flaws present since early 2024 in release lines 3.13.0 and later. CVE-2026-57219 (CVSS 8.7) exposes the OAuth client secret via an obsolete GET /api/auth endpoint, enabling full broker takeover, while CVE-2026-57221 (CVSS 5.3) lets any authenticated user enumerate cross-tenant queue metadata. Patch to 4.3.0, 4.2.6, 4.1.11, 4.0.20, or 3.13.15, restrict port 15672, and rotate exposed secrets.

title: 'The bots are alive!' Jailbroken Gemini spun up new C2 server for Russian fraudster in just 6 minutes
blurb: A jailbroken Google Gemini performed roughly 90% of the work in a credential- and cryptocurrency-theft operation run by a solo Russian-speaking actor known as "bandcampro," spinning up a new C2 server in six minutes and executing 59 unprompted actions during a botnet migration, per a TrendAI report. The analysis of 200-plus Gemini CLI session logs also documents C2 payloads hidden via invisible prompt injection, which TrendAI argues renders signature-based artifact scanning insufficient against AI-driven infrastructure.

title: SpaceXAI’s Grok programming tool was uploading its users’ entire codebase to cloud storage
blurb: SpaceXAI's Grok Build CLI was uploading users' entire code repositories to Google Cloud, including files it was told not to open and secrets deleted from Git history, per findings published by Cereblab. SpaceXAI has since disabled the behavior (its servers now return a "disable_codebase_upload: true" flag) and Musk says previously synced data will be deleted, but the exposed data could include proprietary source, credentials, and infrastructure details, so treat any secrets synced by the tool as compromised and rotate them.

title: Cursor IDE Auto-Executes Malicious Code in Poisoned Repos
blurb: Mindgard disclosed an unpatched vulnerability in the Cursor AI IDE that auto-executes a malicious "git.exe" planted at a repository root, running arbitrary code with the developer's privileges the moment a poisoned project is opened. Cursor's path-resolution logic searches the workspace itself for Git binaries and runs one with no warning or approval; the flaw has remained exploitable since Mindgard first reported it in December after Cursor declined to engage on coordinated disclosure.

title: Researchers Say Claude for Chrome Flaw Lets Rogue Extensions Trigger Gmail Reads
blurb: Manifold Security disclosed a flaw in Claude for Chrome v1.0.80 that lets any extension running a script on claude.ai dispatch a synthetic click to trigger allowlisted tasks that read a user's Gmail, Google Docs, and Calendar, because the handler never checks event.isTrusted. No patch exists as of July 14; disable "Act without asking" mode, which otherwise runs forged tasks with no approval prompt, and audit any extension permitted to read or change data on claude.ai.

title: How I Turned AI to the Dark Side
blurb: Security researcher Dave Kuszmar disclosed a set of prompt-based techniques that bypass safety guardrails across nearly every major LLM, extracting detailed instructions for producing napalm, methamphetamine, and weapons-grade uranium, including through a Gemini-backed Darth Vader character in Fortnite. He argues the restrictions meant to secure these models are themselves the attack surface, and that labs have been largely unresponsive to his disclosures.

title: US charges alleged operators of Russian bulletproof hosting service
blurb: US federal prosecutors unsealed charges against three Russian nationals — Aleksandr Volosovik ("Yalishanda"), Yulia Pankova, and Kirill Zatolokin — accusing them of running the Media Land and ML.Cloud bulletproof hosting services that supplied infrastructure to LockBit, BlackSuit, and Play, causing over $62 million in damage. The State Department's Rewards for Justice program is now offering up to $10 million for information on government-linked associates of the defendants.

title: Microsoft Entra ID gets passkeys default authentication starting September
blurb: Microsoft will make passkeys the default authentication method for Entra ID starting September 2026, auto-enrolling users currently on SMS and voice MFA before retiring Microsoft-provided telephony delivery for those methods on February 1, 2027. Admins should run the Entra SMS/Voice Policy Scanner PowerShell script to identify affected users and migrate them to phishing-resistant methods now, since threat actors like ShinyHunters have heavily targeted Entra SSO accounts and AI-driven phishing is hitting 54% click-through rates against phishable second factors.

title: When Binaries Talk Back: Representation-Confusion Attacks on LLM-Assisted Reverse Engineering
blurb: This paper introduces Representation-Confusion Attacks in Reverse Engineering (RARE), where attacker-controlled binaries trick LLM-assisted RE pipelines into treating extracted data as trusted instructions or independent evidence, causing the tested models to propose planted unsafe actions in 35 of 40 adversarial cases. The authors' RARE-Guard controls stop it, with Tool Authorization denying all unsafe proposals and a Provenance Gate that groups same-origin records before counting support, cutting false-claim validation from 32/32 to 0.

title: Trust but Verify? Uncovering the Security Debt of Autonomous Coding Agents
blurb: A large-scale study using the AIDev dataset found that 38.9% of agent-generated pull requests contain at least one security smell, with supply-chain integrity issues making up 82.3% and hard-coded credentials accounting for 99.6% of critical-severity findings. Notably, human collaborators introduced 67.6% of genuine leaked secrets, and existing review processes missed 81.1% before merge. The authors argue for context-aware guardrails at the point of human-AI collaboration.

title: Accelerating EDR Evasion with LLM-Driven Analysis
blurb: SpecterOps researchers detailed using LLMs to accelerate reverse-engineering of the "big 5" EDR products, driving disassembly and debugging to surface on-host evasion techniques far faster than manual analysis. The post argues LLM-driven analysis is systematically breaking on-host detections and urges vendors and defenders to assume their endpoint controls are more evadable than believed. Red teams should expect these bypasses to become public rather than trade-secret, shifting defensive weight toward behavioral and network telemetry that survives host-level evasion.

title: New phishing kits target Microsoft 365 accounts, evade MFA
blurb: Two new phishing kits, Jalisco and OmegaLord, target Microsoft 365 accounts with techniques that defeat MFA, per ReliaQuest: Jalisco generates fresh OAuth device codes in real time to beat Microsoft's 15-minute validity window, while OmegaLord poses as a PDF reader to harvest credentials and phone numbers for MFA interception. After compromise, attackers register up to five rogue devices, exfiltrate SharePoint and SaaS data in as little as six minutes, and follow with extortion.

title: Skills That Don't Exist: A Large-Scale Study of Hallucinated Skill Recommendation in LLM Agents
blurb: A large-scale study of LLM agents found that when asked to recommend installable skills from open registries, every tested configuration invents names for skills that exist nowhere, averaging 36% hallucination and rising to 43.1% on real developer questions. Because these fake names repeat consistently across models and registries rarely verify publishers, an attacker can pre-register malicious skills under the predicted names and wait for agents to install the payload.

title: Antiproof: Synthesizing Vulnerability Detectors and Proofs of Exploitability
blurb: Antiproof is an end-to-end vulnerability discovery system that pairs neuro-symbolic detector synthesis for high recall with proof-of-exploitability oracles that validate candidates by executing concrete attacker capabilities. It detected 64 of 66 vulnerabilities on BountyBench and KEVBench, over 60 points better recall than baselines, and surfaced several hundred unknown bugs across 50 systems. The authors have 12 CVE assignments so far, including RCE in Ray, SGLang, vLLM, and LiteLLM.

title: Blocking Slow-Burn Attacks: Contextual Policies in Omnigent
blurb: Databricks demonstrates a contextual policy in Omnigent that blocks indirect prompt-injection "slow-burn" attacks, where a malicious goal is split into individually benign steps (read a doc, read another, summarize, send) that no single-action guardrail catches. The stateful policy tracks accumulated risk across the whole session and blocks the outbound step once the agent has read too much sensitive material. The guard is tamper-resistant: the agent has no tool to disable it, adding policies requires human approval, and any denial wins when policies combine.

title: AutoTrace: From Patches to Triggers via Agentic Interprocedural Exploration
blurb: AutoTrace is an agentic pipeline that localizes vulnerability triggers by walking a code property graph layer by layer, using LLM agents to decide where to look while deterministic admissibility gates require graph-backed evidence before any trigger is reported. It hit 75.0% VulnHit and 80.8% FuncHit on the InterPVD benchmark, beating prior state of the art, and the authors released SinkTrace-Bench, 1,542 balanced source-to-sink samples on which even frontier LLMs struggle to separate matched vulnerable and patched pairs.

title: LabubaRAT Masquerades as NVIDIA Software to Control Windows Hosts
blurb: Blackpoint Cyber documented LabubaRAT, a previously undocumented Rust-based RAT that ships as "nvidia-sysruntime.exe" to impersonate NVIDIA's container runtime and take hands-on control of Windows hosts, fingerprinting installed EDR (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender) before running commands, capturing screenshots, and proxying traffic. It accepts C2 config at runtime rather than hard-coding it and falls back across HTTPS, WebView2, and DNS tunneling, so defenders should hunt for the binary and block the pipicka[.]xyz infrastructure.

title: Baddies caught exploiting extensions bugs with perfect 10 scores on vulnerable Joomla websites
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting critical flaws (CVSS 10.0) in third-party Joomla extensions to compromise vulnerable websites, according to The Register. Site operators running affected extensions should apply vendor patches immediately, audit for webshells and unauthorized admin accounts, and treat any exposed installation as potentially breached given the perfect-score, unauthenticated nature of the bugs.

title: Iran abused mobile networks’ vulnerabilities to locate US military in the Middle East, report says
blurb: Iran's government exploited long-known SS7 signaling flaws in 2G and 3G telecom networks to geolocate U.S. military personnel across Iraq, Bahrain, and other Middle East countries during the buildup to the Iran War, according to Financial Times reporting on research by the Mobile Surveillance Monitor. The tracking reportedly guided strikes on bases and hotels that caused several injuries, and Iran paired SS7 abuse with ad-tech location targeting, another commodity surveillance technique.

title: Upwind links compromise of multiple AsyncAPI npm packages to coordinated attack on software release process
blurb: Upwind disclosed a coordinated supply-chain campaign against the AsyncAPI npm ecosystem, in which attackers compromised multiple GitHub repositories, targeted separate release branches, and abused several OIDC publishing identities to push malicious code through legitimate release channels. The payload executed during normal package imports rather than via preinstall or postinstall scripts, so defenders monitoring only installation activity should shift to runtime and import-time detection and audit OIDC publishing trust for affected packages.

title: US, Allies Warn of Russian Cyberattacks Targeting Critical Infrastructure Routers
blurb: A coalition of 12 governments including the US, UK, and Czech Republic warned that Russian FSB Center 16 actors (Berserk Bear, Static Tundra, Energetic Bear) are compromising edge routers across energy, defense, government, and healthcare networks by abusing SNMP set-requests to exfiltrate device configs and exploiting old Cisco flaws CVE-2008-4128 and CVE-2018-0171. Defenders should disable Cisco Smart Install, drop SNMPv1/v2 for SNMPv3 with modern encryption, and monitor for local-account logins on network devices.

title: SingGuard-NSFA: Open-source guardrails for agentic AI
blurb: SingGuard-NSFA is an open-source guardrail framework for agentic AI, shipping four Qwen3.5-based models (0.8B to 9B) that classify agent inputs and outputs against a 185-variant risk taxonomy spanning prompt injection, tool abuse, and data leakage. Its real-time mode runs per-domain classification heads in 45 to 57 milliseconds per sample, and teams can add new risk categories by training lightweight heads on the frozen backbone without retraining.

title: SAP Patches Critical Vulnerabilities in NetWeaver, Approuter, Commerce Cloud
blurb: SAP patched 20 security notes on its July 2026 patch day, led by CVE-2026-44747 (CVSS 9.9), a memory corruption bug in NetWeaver Application Server ABAP that allows attackers to read, modify, and disrupt system data. Two other critical flaws affect Approuter (CVE-2026-27690, HTTP request smuggling) and Commerce Cloud (CVE-2026-44761, hardcoded OAuth2 credentials in sample scripts); apply patches and audit production for the default credentials.

title: Bulkhead: Automated Semantic Detection and Remediation of Container Escape Vulnerabilities
blurb: This paper introduces Bulkhead, a framework that pairs LLMs with formal methods to automatically detect and patch path-traversal (PaTra) container-escape flaws caused by cross-boundary path misresolution between host and container. It targets vulnerabilities that have grown more common as cloud systems mount shared GPUs and agent workspaces into containers for AI workloads, using multi-agent call-chain analysis to generate validating PoCs and assertion-verified patches.

title: I tricked Claude into leaking your deepest, darkest secrets
blurb: A security researcher demonstrated exfiltrating a Claude.ai user's stored memory—full name, employer, and security-question answers—by chaining a prompt injection with Claude's built-in web_fetch tool to smuggle the data to an attacker-controlled server. The attack abuses Claude's daily memory-summarization pass and conversation_search retrieval, turning a high-fidelity personal profile into a source for blackmail, impersonation, or security-question bypass with no visible indication to the victim.

title: 'Ghostcommit' hides prompt injection in images to fool AI agents, steal secrets
blurb: Researchers disclosed "Ghostcommit," an attack that hides prompt-injection instructions inside a PNG so AI code reviewers, which treat images as opaque binary blobs, wave the malicious pull request through. Once merged, an AGENTS.md convention points a coding agent to the image, which instructs it to read .env and emit the repository's secrets as an encoded integer constant; CodeRabbit, Bugbot, and Cursor driving Claude Sonnet all missed or executed it.

title: Microsoft discloses ‘the mother of all’ vulnerability loads, tripling June’s previous record
blurb: Microsoft's July Patch Tuesday addressed 622 vulnerabilities, tripling June's record 206 and including two actively exploited zero-days: CVE-2026-56155 and CVE-2026-56164, privilege-escalation flaws in Active Directory Federation Services and SharePoint Server. The surge stems from Microsoft's agentic scanning harness MDASH; 63 defects were rated critical, but volume reflects tooling reach rather than exploitable risk, so prioritize the two zero-days and critical Windows patches first.

title: A broken DNSSEC rollover took down .AL. Now 1.1.1.1 tells you when validation is bypassed
blurb: A broken DNSSEC key rollover at Albania's .AL registry on July 3 caused validation failures that rendered every .AL domain, including government, banking, and media sites, unreachable to validating resolvers. Cloudflare restored resolution on 1.1.1.1 by installing a Negative Trust Anchor to bypass DNSSEC for .AL, the same fix it used for .DE two months earlier. This time it also returned a new Extended DNS Error code on affected responses, so clients can tell when an answer was served unvalidated rather than silently trusting it.

title: On the Security Implications of PQC in TLS: Handshake Exhaustion and IDS Degradation
blurb: Researchers show that integrating post-quantum cryptography into TLS 1.3 amplifies handshake exhaustion DDoS attacks, prolonging sustained high server CPU utilization by up to 88x and turning a moderate flood into a severe outage. In their testbed, deep-learning IDS also went blind to the attacks under PQC traffic, with exosphere dropping to ~50% recall and HyperVision's AU-ROC degrading to near-random 0.49.

title: Nearly 300 GitHub repos pose as legit software to push malware
blurb: Arctic Wolf uncovered 292 fake GitHub repositories impersonating security, crypto, financial, and developer software to distribute a BoryptGrab infostealer variant via DLL side-loading, where a signed WinGUP updater loads a trojanized libcurl.dll that runs the payload in memory. The malware drains credentials from 19 browsers, 32 crypto wallets, Telegram, Discord, and Steam sessions, so treat GitHub-sourced binaries as untrusted and hunt for gup.exe side-loading unsigned libcurl.dll.

title: Adobe Patches Critical ColdFusion Vulnerabilities
blurb: Adobe patched 88 vulnerabilities across 12 products on Tuesday, including eight critical ColdFusion bugs (CVE-2026-48318 and others) enabling arbitrary code execution and privilege escalation via path traversal, SQL injection, and missing authentication. The priority 1 advisory follows a ColdFusion fix two weeks prior that attackers exploited within hours of disclosure, so admins should apply ColdFusion 2025 update 11 or 2023 update 22 immediately.

title: Musk promises purge after Grok Build caught sending entire repos to the cloud
blurb: xAI's Grok Build coding assistant was found uploading entire repositories to xAI's cloud during indexing, exposing proprietary source and any embedded secrets to servers users hadn't authorized. Elon Musk pledged to purge the collected data and disable the behavior, but teams that ran Grok Build against private repos should assume their code and hardcoded credentials already left the perimeter and rotate affected secrets.

title: Guardian Angels: LLM Personalization for Productivity and Security
blurb: Gwern proposes "Guardian Angels": personalized digital-twin LLMs that emulate a single user's values and preferences rather than acting as generic assistants, unifying principal and agent to sidestep the principal-agent problem. The security case is that a GA hardwired to one situated user resists prompt-injection and confused-deputy attacks, and can screen incoming messages for synthetic-media propaganda and spearphishing as part of a defense-in-depth strategy.

title: MindReader: Using LLMs to Encourage Memorable and Secure Password Replacement
blurb: MindReader is a tool that uses LLMs to help users pick forced-replacement passwords that are harder to guess but still memorable, by inferring the meaning behind an old password's components and suggesting semantically related but syntactically unrelated substitutes. In a user study, MindReader passwords resisted online guessing better than both self-chosen replacements and originals, even against an attacker who knew the old password and the tool's full implementation, while remaining comparably memorable a week later.

title: “Context bombs” can frustrate AI-driven attacks, researchers found
blurb: Tracebit researchers found that planting "context bombs" — short text designed to trip the safety guardrails of offensive AI agents — inside canary secrets sharply degraded autonomous attacks across five leading models over 152 runs. Opus 4.8 reached full admin in 93% of clean runs but failed every time once a bomb was present, and Gemini 3.1 Pro lost its 60% admin-plus-persistence rate. The tactic turns decoy credentials into a defense that stalls AI agents long enough for responders to react.

title: ClickFix's Mushrooming Ecosystem Demands New Defense Tactics
blurb: ReversingLabs reports that ClickFix has industrialized into a malware-as-a-service ecosystem, with rentable attack kits that push info-stealers like Lumma via user-pasted PowerShell commands, generating few of the signals AV and EDR are tuned to catch. Because execution runs through legitimate tools and trusted user behavior, the researchers recommend YARA-based structural analysis of the HTML delivery pages as the most reliable detection point, and published a rule defenders can deploy.

title: SAP warns of critical flaws in NetWeaver and Commerce Cloud
blurb: SAP patched 16 vulnerabilities in its July 2026 updates, including three critical flaws: an out-of-bounds write in NetWeaver AS ABAP (CVE-2026-44747), HTTP request smuggling in AppRouter (CVE-2026-27690), and default credentials in Commerce Cloud (CVE-2026-44761) that grant valid access tokens. No exploitation is confirmed yet, but CISA has added 14 SAP flaws to its KEV catalog since 2021, so prioritize patching internet-facing instances.

title: Centers Laboratory Data Breach Affects 540,000 Individuals
blurb: Centers Laboratory, a New Jersey healthcare diagnostics provider, disclosed a breach affecting 542,377 individuals after WorldLeaks actors accessed its systems between August 9 and 14, 2025 and exfiltrated names, SSNs, driver's license and passport numbers, and medical and health insurance data. WorldLeaks, the extortion-only successor to Hunters International, leaked over 1.6 million files totaling 720 GB after listing the org in October 2025.

title: Mystra: Declarative Dynamic Taint Analysis via Shadow Virtual Machine
blurb: Researchers introduced Mystra, a declarative taint-specification language backed by a Shadow Virtual Machine that runs alongside host runtimes to track multi-level taint and provenance without engine-specific instrumentation. Its V8 implementation hit 95.5% recall with zero false positives across 493 in-scope Node.js CVEs, at 1.85x runtime overhead. For teams building DTA into JavaScript or Python pipelines, the rules compile ahead of time and are designed for validated LLM-assisted synthesis.

title: Critical Vulnerabilities Patched With Fresh Chrome 150, Firefox 152 Updates
blurb: Mozilla shipped Firefox 152.0.6 to fix two critical flaws, an invalid-pointer bug in WebAssembly (CVE-2026-15718) and a site-isolation issue in DOM Navigation (CVE-2026-15719), both with public exploit code but no confirmed in-the-wild abuse. Google's Chrome 150 patches 15 bugs, including two critical use-after-free defects in Ozone (CVE-2026-15764, CVE-2026-15765), so update both browsers now.

title: Apple says former employee exploited ‘rare' bug to download confidential files after leaving for OpenAI
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI over trade-secret theft, alleging former electrical engineer Chang Liu exploited a zero-day authentication bug to siphon dozens of confidential hardware files from shared network folders weeks after leaving for OpenAI. Apple has patched the flaw and cited server logs showing Liu was the only one of a "few" with potential access who exploited it, underscoring the risk of incomplete offboarding and undecommissioned credentials.

title: Cross-Cutting Security Analysis of LLM-Generated Code via Metamorphic Testing and Association Rule Mining
blurb: This paper tests security in LLM-generated code using metamorphic relations plus association rule mining, applying nine relations across major CWE categories to 3,700 snippets from five open models. 68.8% violated at least one relation, led by hard-coded credentials (79.1%) and command injection (74.4%), and violations cluster: XSS plus weak crypto predicts hard-coded credentials with 82.5% confidence. The takeaway for defenders is cluster-aware verification, since database- and auth-related prompts reliably produce broad cross-cutting insecurity.

title: 6 GHz Wi-Fi Flaws Could Disrupt Critical Systems
blurb: Researchers from Penn State and Idaho National Laboratory disclosed design flaws in the Automated Frequency Coordination (AFC) systems that govern 6 GHz Wi-Fi, which trust unauthenticated client-side inputs like GPS, GNSS, DNS, and NTP by default. Off-path attackers can spoof location or time data to force incorrect frequency and power assignments, causing interference with incumbent radio, cellular backhaul, and public-safety networks, or denial-of-service for 6 GHz clients.

title: Open-Source Intelligence for Code Provenance and the Security Patterns that Separate Human and Large-Language-Model Implementations of Common Programming Tasks
blurb: This paper builds a fully reproducible, open-source pipeline that collects human and LLM implementations of 31 security-sensitive tasks, including OAuth PKCE, JWT verification, and password hashing, then trains a classifier that recovers human-versus-model provenance at 93% accuracy. The two sources diverge on security patterns in every language tested. A repair case study hit a 77% fix rate but repeatedly showed models stripping the insecure pattern without adding the correct defense, leaving code superficially clean but still exposed.

title: PVDetector: Detecting Prompt Injection Attacks on Purpose-Specific LLM Agents through Policy-Violation Concept Analysis
blurb: This paper introduces PVDetector, a training-free framework that detects prompt injection against purpose-specific LLM agents by measuring hidden-state alignment with "policy-violation" concepts derived offline from contrastive pairs of compliant and violating prompts. The authors find that LLMs retain latent awareness of requests that exceed their designated role, and that reading the activation space beats input-output pattern analysis. Across multiple models and datasets, PVDetector reports a sub-1% false-negative rate with minimal inference overhead, outperforming prior detection methods.

title: Records Are Made to Be Broken: Patch Tuesday Raises Triage Stakes
blurb: Microsoft's July 2026 Patch Tuesday fixes 622 unique CVEs, the largest release in the program's history, including three zero-days (two under active exploitation) and more than 60 critical flaws. Prioritize the exploited bugs and a publicly disclosed BitLocker flaw over raw volume, starting with the 13 vulnerabilities rated CVSS 9.8 and any exposed AD FS infrastructure.

title: Hands off our VPNs, privacy groups tell UK ministers
blurb: Privacy and civil-liberties groups urged UK ministers to abandon proposals that would restrict or ban consumer VPNs, warning the measures would weaken encryption and undermine online privacy for legitimate users. The pushback follows Online Safety Act age-verification rules that have driven a spike in VPN adoption, with campaigners arguing that curbing the tools would harm security without stopping the harms regulators cite.

title: Explainable-by-Design Audio Deepfake Detection via Wiener-Hopf Linear Prediction
blurb: This paper proposes an audio deepfake detector built on Wiener-Hopf linear prediction feeding a lightweight 2D CNN, trading black-box complexity for an explainable-by-design pipeline that matches state-of-the-art accuracy at lower compute cost. Grad-CAM analysis shows the classifier keys on low-order predictor coefficients and silence or transitional regions, exposing reverberation artifacts and statistical inconsistencies in synthetic speech. Fine-tuning restores detection under additive noise, MP3 compression, and telephone filtering, making it viable for forensics teams reviewing degraded real-world audio.

title: Explaining Intrusion Alert Decisions of Deep Learning-based Network Intrusion Detection Systems for Security Analysts
blurb: This paper introduces EXP-SEC, a framework that explains why deep-learning-based network intrusion detection systems fire alerts, translating model feature importances into SOC-analyst-readable justifications. It adds a forensic module to isolate the suspect packets or flows behind an alert and handles overlapping, unequally weighted feature groups that prior methods flatten. In evaluation against xNIDS, the current best explanation framework, EXP-SEC scored higher on group-level and overlap-aware utility metrics while matching it on descriptive accuracy, sparsity, and stability.

title: Spanish Police take down €140 million cyber fraud ring, arrest four
blurb: Spanish police dismantled a cybercrime and money-laundering ring that netted €140M ($160M) from investment fraud and business email compromise, arresting four suspects in Spain, Portugal, and Panama. Investigators traced €94M through a network of over 800 bank accounts and 67 money mules, froze €3M for victims, and seized 15 computers and 170 smartphones used to run thousands of fraudulent transfers.

title: LastPass, Bitwarden users targeted with fake security alerts
blurb: LastPass and Bitwarden users are being targeted by a phishing campaign that sends fake security-policy notices from lookalike domains (lastpassnewsletter.com, bitwardennewsletter.com), routing victims through DocuSign-impersonating landing pages at lastpasscompliance[.]com and bitwardencompliance[.]com to download a Windows/macOS payload. LastPass confirmed its systems were not breached and reminds users it never requests master passwords; block the flagged domains and have anyone who entered credentials reset their master password from a trusted device.

title: OpenAI hides Codex agent instructions behind encryption, leaving developers in the dark
blurb: OpenAI began encrypting its Codex agent's internal MultiAgentV2 instructions, hiding the messages agents pass between one another from the developers running them. The change strips visibility that teams relied on for debugging and auditing agent behavior, leaving no way to inspect what the model was actually told before it acted.

title: Patchpocalypse Now: Microsoft tops last month's record with 622 Patch Tuesday CVEs
blurb: Microsoft's July Patch Tuesday shipped fixes for 622 CVEs, surpassing last month's record, including patches for on-prem SharePoint flaws that Redmond's earlier updates failed to fully remediate and that are now under active zero-day exploitation. Prioritize the SharePoint fixes and any actively exploited or critical RCE entries first, and verify the SharePoint patches actually apply given prior incomplete remediation before treating exposed servers as covered.

title: Microsoft’s biggest ever patch Tuesday lets you hold off updating for longer
blurb: Microsoft's July 2026 Patch Tuesday fixed at least 570 security holes, nearly triple last month's record and its largest release ever, with the company crediting AI-assisted vulnerability discovery for the surge. The same build (KB5101650) adds an option to pause updates for up to 35 days and extend indefinitely, giving defenders a longer patch-deferral window on a month with an unusually large remediation backlog.

title: AWS Security Hub expands coverage to Microsoft Azure and beefs up AI protections
blurb: AWS made Security Hub's multicloud management generally available for Microsoft Azure, letting it natively discover and evaluate Azure VMs, containers, function apps, and identities for misconfigurations, vulnerabilities, and internet exposure against the CIS Azure Foundations Benchmark. AWS also added GuardDuty AI Protection and an AI asset inventory, surfacing Azure findings in the same format, automation, and response workflows as AWS alerts.

title: Synopsys Finds No Evidence of Data Breach Amid Bosch Hack Claims
blurb: Synopsys said it found no evidence of a data breach after a new ransomware group called D1R claimed to have exploited a website vulnerability to steal a 40,000-entry corporate client database and pivot into customer Bosch's intellectual property. A screenshot the group posted as proof of the Bosch compromise appears to come from a user manual already in the public domain, and Synopsys says the threat actor never contacted it.

title: Cribl buys AI-native threat detection engineering startup CardinalOps
